pages.text: $(italic)Supplementaries'$() $(bold)Wind Vane$() is the one block in Novus that puts the weather onto a wire. Mount it up where the wind can reach it; when a storm rolls over its position it turns, and sends out a $(bold)redstone signal$() — stronger in a thunderstorm than in plain rain.$(br2)Wire it to a door, a shutter, a bell — anything you'd like the sky to decide.
pages.title: The Wind Vane

pages.text: $(bold)Why the vane, and not a daylight sensor.$() Novus ships $(italic)Simple Clouds Compat$() so the Wind Vane reads Simple Clouds' $(italic)local$() storms at its own position — the storm actually overhead, not a world-wide flag.$(br2)$(bold)$(novus)A daylight detector will not do this.$()$() It reads the global sky-darkening, which Simple Clouds leaves untouched — so it ignores the storm above it and only tracks the sun. For sensing rain in Novus, the Wind Vane is the tool; the daylight sensor stays a clock.
pages.title: Why the Vane

pages.text: $(bold)And the sculk?$() A $(italic)Sculk Sensor$() cannot hear rain — falling rain makes no vibration for it to catch. What it $(italic)can$() catch is a $(bold)lightning strike$(): during a thunderstorm, each bolt that lands is a vibration the sculk will feel. A sensor on a tall mast is a fair lightning-warning, but it is listening for the strike, not the weather.
pages.title: And the Sculk
